cards.title: Drawing structure of molecules & polyatomic ions
cards.questionImage: Reference/board-01 drawing-structure-of-molecules-polyatomic-ions Drawing structure of molecules & polyatomic ions [Q].png
cards.answerImage: Reference/board-01 drawing-structure-of-molecules-polyatomic-ions Drawing structure of molecules & polyatomic ions [ans].png
cards.transcriptFile: Reference/board-01 drawing-structure-of-molecules-polyatomic-ions Drawing structure of molecules & polyatomic ions [text].txt
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-01 drawing-structure-of-molecules-polyatomic-ions Drawing structure of molecules & polyatomic ions.md

extensionQuestions.question: According to the step-by-step method, how should positive and negative charges be initially distributed among the atoms?
extensionQuestions.answer: Positive charges (+) are placed on the electro-positive atom, and negative charges (-) are placed on the electronegative atom.

cards.title: VSEPR
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-01 vsepr VSEPR.md

extensionQuestions.question: What is the molecular geometry and bond angle of ClF₃, which has 3 bond pairs and 2 lone pairs around the central chlorine atom?
extensionQuestions.answer: The molecular geometry is T-shape and the bond angle is 90°.

extensionQuestions.question: What is the molecular geometry and bond angle of the IF₂⁻ ion, which has 2 bond pairs and 3 lone pairs around the central iodine atom?
extensionQuestions.answer: The molecular geometry is linear and the bond angle is 180°.

cards.title: Application of VSEPR
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-02 application-of-vsepr-2 Application of VSEPR.md

extensionQuestions.question: State the fundamental principle of VSEPR theory regarding the spatial arrangement of electron pairs around a central atom?
extensionQuestions.answer: Electron pairs (both bond pairs and lone pairs) around the central atom arrange themselves as far apart as possible in space to minimize mutual repulsion.

extensionQuestions.question: What is the relative order of repulsion strength between lone pairs (lp) and bond pairs (bp) of electrons according to VSEPR theory?
extensionQuestions.answer: lone pair-lone pair (lp-lp) repulsion > lone pair-bond pair (lp-bp) repulsion > bond pair-bond pair (bp-bp) repulsion

cards.title: Example 2
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-02 example-2 Example 2.md

extensionQuestions.question: How does the electronegativity difference between fluorine and hydrogen affect the position of the bonding electron pairs in NF₃ compared to NH₃?
extensionQuestions.answer: Fluorine is more electronegative than hydrogen, so it attracts the bonding electron pairs closer to itself in NF₃, whereas the bond pairs remain closer to the nitrogen atom in NH₃.

extensionQuestions.question: Explain why the repulsion between bond pairs is greater in NH₃ than in NF₃, and state its effect on the bond angle?
extensionQuestions.answer: Because the bond pairs of electrons are closer together around the central nitrogen atom in NH₃, there is greater repulsion between these bond pairs, which makes the bond angle in NH₃ greater than in NF₃.

cards.title: Example 1
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-02 example-1 Example 1.md

extensionQuestions.question: What shape and bond angle are expected for NH₃?
extensionQuestions.answer: NH₃ is trigonal pyramidal with a bond angle of about 107°.

cards.title: Example 3
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-02 example-3 Example 3.md

extensionQuestions.question: Why can a molecule with a very small O-O-O angle be unstable?
extensionQuestions.answer: The bonding pairs are forced too close together, causing additional repulsion.

cards.title: Structure and Bonding
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-02 structure-and-bonding-2 Structure and Bonding.md

extensionQuestions.question: What should be linked when explaining structure and bonding?
extensionQuestions.answer: The type of bonding, structure, particles present, and forces of attraction should be linked.

extensionQuestions.question: Why do different structures have different physical properties?
extensionQuestions.answer: They contain different particles and different strengths of attraction that require different amounts of energy to overcome.

cards.title: Giant molecular structure
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-02 giant-molecular-structure Giant molecular structure.md

extensionQuestions.question: What type of bonding holds atoms together in a giant molecular structure?
extensionQuestions.answer: Strong covalent bonds hold atoms together throughout the giant structure.

extensionQuestions.question: Why do giant molecular structures usually have high melting points?
extensionQuestions.answer: Many strong covalent bonds must be broken, requiring a large amount of energy.

cards.title: Simple molecular structure
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-02 simple-molecular-structure Simple molecular structure.md

extensionQuestions.question: What forces are overcome when a simple molecular substance melts or boils?
extensionQuestions.answer: Weak intermolecular forces between molecules are overcome.

cards.title: Giant metallic lattice
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-02 giant-metallic-lattice Giant metallic lattice.md

extensionQuestions.question: Why can metals conduct electricity?
extensionQuestions.answer: Delocalised electrons are mobile and can carry charge through the structure.

cards.title: Giant ionic lattice
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-02 giant-ionic-lattice Giant ionic lattice.md

extensionQuestions.question: Why do giant ionic compounds usually have high melting points?
extensionQuestions.answer: Strong electrostatic attractions between oppositely charged ions require a large amount of energy to overcome.

cards.title: Further classification of bonds
cards.questionImage: Reference/board-03 further-classification-of-bonds Further classification of bonds [Q].png
cards.answerImage: Reference/board-03 further-classification-of-bonds Further classification of bonds [ans].png
cards.transcriptFile: Reference/board-03 further-classification-of-bonds Further classification of bonds [text].txt
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-03 further-classification-of-bonds Further classification of bonds.md

extensionQuestions.question: What bond types are classified in this section?
extensionQuestions.answer: Sigma and pi bonds, dative covalent bonds, polar bonds, and ionic bonds with covalent character are classified.

extensionQuestions.question: What should be identified before explaining a bond classification?
extensionQuestions.answer: Identify the electron sharing or donation, electronegativity difference, and any polarisation of electron cloud.

cards.title: sigma and pi bonds
cards.questionImage: Reference/board-03 sigma-and-pi-bonds sigma and pi bonds [Q].png
cards.answerImage: Reference/board-03 sigma-and-pi-bonds sigma and pi bonds [ans].png
cards.transcriptFile: Reference/board-03 sigma-and-pi-bonds sigma and pi bonds [text].txt
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-03 sigma-and-pi-bonds sigma and pi bonds.md

extensionQuestions.question: How is a σ bond formed?
extensionQuestions.answer: A σ bond is formed by head-on overlap of orbitals.

extensionQuestions.question: How is a π bond formed?
extensionQuestions.answer: A π bond is formed by side-on overlap of p orbitals.

cards.title: polar bonds
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-03 polar-bonds polar bonds.md

extensionQuestions.question: What causes a covalent bond to be polar?
extensionQuestions.answer: A difference in electronegativity causes the bonding electrons to be shared unequally.

cards.title: dative covalent bond
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-03 dative-covalent-bond dative covalent bond.md

extensionQuestions.question: What is a dative covalent bond?
extensionQuestions.answer: It is a covalent bond in which both bonding electrons are donated by one atom.

extensionQuestions.question: What must the donor atom have to form a dative bond?
extensionQuestions.answer: The donor atom must have a lone pair of electrons.

cards.title: ionic bond with covalent character
cards.questionImage: Reference/board-03 ionic-bond-with-covalent-character ionic bond with covalent character [Q].png
cards.answerImage: Reference/board-03 ionic-bond-with-covalent-character ionic bond with covalent character [ans].png
cards.transcriptFile: Reference/board-03 ionic-bond-with-covalent-character ionic bond with covalent character [text].txt
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-03 ionic-bond-with-covalent-character ionic bond with covalent character.md

extensionQuestions.question: What two factors affect the extent of polarisation?
extensionQuestions.answer: The charge density of the cation and the size or polarisability of the anion.

cards.title: Intermolecular forces of attraction (IMF)
cards.questionImage: Reference/board-03 intermolecular-forces-of-attraction-imf Intermolecular forces of attraction (IMF) [Q].png
cards.answerImage: Reference/board-03 intermolecular-forces-of-attraction-imf Intermolecular forces of attraction (IMF) [ans].png
cards.transcriptFile: Reference/board-03 intermolecular-forces-of-attraction-imf Intermolecular forces of attraction (IMF) [text].txt
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-03 intermolecular-forces-of-attraction-imf Intermolecular forces of attraction (IMF).md

extensionQuestions.question: What are intermolecular forces?
extensionQuestions.answer: They are forces of attraction between molecules.

extensionQuestions.question: How do stronger intermolecular forces affect boiling point?
extensionQuestions.answer: Stronger intermolecular forces increase boiling point because more energy is required to overcome them.

cards.title: id-id
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-03 id-id id-id.md

extensionQuestions.question: How do instantaneous dipole-induced dipole interactions arise?
extensionQuestions.answer: Electrons are constantly moving. At any given moment, electron density in a molecule can be unsymmetrical, resulting in an instantaneous dipole which can then induce a dipole on a neighbouring molecule.

extensionQuestions.question: How does molecular size affect id-id interactions?
extensionQuestions.answer: Larger molecules have larger and more polarisable electron clouds, hence have stronger id-id interactions.

cards.title: H-bond
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-03 h-bond H-bond.md

extensionQuestions.question: When can hydrogen bonding occur?
extensionQuestions.answer: Hydrogen bonding occurs when H is bonded to N, O, or F and is attracted to a lone pair on N, O, or F.

cards.title: pd-pd
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-03 pd-pd pd-pd.md

extensionQuestions.question: Which molecules can experience pd-pd interactions?
extensionQuestions.answer: Polar molecules with permanent dipoles can experience pd-pd interactions.

cards.title: solubility
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-03 solubility solubility.md

extensionQuestions.question: State the type of interaction formed between Na⁺ and H₂O?
extensionQuestions.answer: ion-dipole interactions

cards.title: Melting/ Boiling
cards.extensionFile: extension question/board-03 melting-boiling Melting Boiling.md

extensionQuestions.question: What must be overcome during boiling of H₂O?
extensionQuestions.answer: H-bonds.

extensionQuestions.question: How does stronger attraction affect melting or boiling point?
extensionQuestions.answer: Stronger attraction increases the melting or boiling point because more energy is required.
